1: The sin of Judah written with an iron pen, engraved with a diamond point on the tablet of their heart — sin inscribed permanently in the heart — establishes the depth of the problem. The NT answer is the new covenant's counter-inscription: "you show that you are a letter from Christ delivered by us, written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts" (2 Corinthians 3:3). What sin writes on the heart with iron, the Spirit rewrites with life.
2: The children remembering their altars and Asherah poles beside every green tree and on the high hills — idolatry taught and practiced across generations — shows the multigenerational transmission of false worship. Jesus addresses this pattern when he says "you nullify the word of God for the sake of your tradition" (Matthew 15:6). The renewal of worship requires not just individual repentance but the breaking of inherited idolatrous patterns — the work the Spirit accomplishes in regenerating communities.
3: The wealth and all the treasures given as plunder, the high places stripped, because of sin throughout the territory — economic loss as covenant consequence — contrasts with Paul's vision of true wealth in Christ: "though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich" (2 Corinthians 8:9). The plundering of material wealth is answered by Christ's enrichment of those who receive him — spiritual wealth that cannot be confiscated.
4: The people letting go of their heritage, to serve enemies in a foreign land, because YHWH's anger has been kindled as a fire that will burn forever — the covenant wrath described as perpetual fire — is the context into which Christ's atonement speaks. The "eternal fire" motif (cf. Isaiah 66:24; Matthew 25:41) is resolved by Christ's bearing of the covenant curse: "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us" (Galatians 3:13). The fire of judgment is absorbed in the one who was made sin for us (2 Corinthians 5:21).
5: Cursed is the man who trusts in man and makes flesh his strength, whose heart turns away from YHWH — this verse is directly engaged in Paul's argument in Galatians 3:10-13. Paul cites Deuteronomy 27:26 (in a parallel argument) to show that all who rely on works of the law are under a curse; Christ has redeemed us from the curse. The "trust in flesh" that Jeremiah diagnoses as the root of covenant failure is precisely the religious self-reliance that Paul declares ineffective for righteousness.
6: The shrub in the desert who shall not see good when it comes — inhabiting the parched places, the salt land — is the image of the man-truster cut off from life and blessing. Paul develops the same contrast in Romans 8:1-11: those who live according to the flesh cannot please God; they are on the path toward spiritual death. The contrast with the tree planted by water in Jeremiah 17:8 is the contrast Paul draws between Spirit-life and flesh-life — the difference being not effort but orientation.
7: Blessed is the man who trusts in YHWH, whose trust is YHWH — the double use of "trust" (batach) establishes that blessed life is defined entirely by the object of one's confidence. This is the OT statement of what faith means in the NT: "whoever believes in him will not be put to shame" (Romans 10:11, citing Isaiah 28:16). The beatitude structure ("blessed is...") connects directly to Jesus's Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-12), which map the same terrain of who is truly blessed.
8: The tree planted by water, sending out its roots by the stream, not fearing when heat comes, bearing fruit even in drought — is the image of the fully flourishing covenant life. Jesus identifies himself as the true vine (John 15:1-11), and Paul's fruit-of-the-Spirit passage (Galatians 5:22-23) describes the same kind of continuous fruitfulness that comes from abiding in Christ. Psalms 1:3 uses identical imagery; Jeremiah and the Psalter together establish that rootedness in YHWH is the source of perpetual fruitfulness.
9: The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately sick — who can understand it? — the most searching anthropological statement in the OT. Paul develops this across Romans 1-3: the heart darkened by idolatry (Romans 1:21-23), the conscience that witnesses but cannot save (Romans 2:15), the universal condemnation (Romans 3:10-18). The answer to the desperately sick heart is not moral effort but the new heart promised in Ezekiel 36:26 — a divine transplant, which Paul identifies as the Spirit's work (2 Corinthians 3:3; Galatians 5:22-23).
10: I YHWH search the heart and test the mind, to give every man according to his ways, according to the fruit of his deeds — divine omniscience applied to judgment. Christ exercises this divine prerogative: "I am he who searches mind and heart, and I will give to each of you according to your works" (Revelation 2:23, Jesus speaking to the seven churches). At the final judgment, Christ is the one who opens the books and examines every heart (2 Corinthians 5:10). The heart-searcher of Jeremiah is identified as Jesus in Revelation.
11: The partridge that hatches eggs it has not laid is the image of wealth gained unjustly — it will leave at the end; the unjust will be a fool. James 5:1-6 applies the same judgment against those who have defrauded workers of their wages. Jesus's parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21) makes the same point: the man who accumulates wealth for himself is not rich toward God. The temporary possession of unjust wealth versus true eternal treasure is a consistent NT theme grounded in this prophetic tradition.
12: A glorious throne set on high from the beginning is the place of our sanctuary — the heavenly throne as the true cultic center, contrasted with earthly temples. Hebrews develops this extensively: the earthly temple was a copy and shadow of the heavenly sanctuary (Hebrews 8:5); Christ has entered the true tent not made with hands (Hebrews 9:11-12); the throne of grace is where believers now approach boldly (Hebrews 4:16). The "glorious throne from the beginning" is the heavenly court where Christ is now seated (Hebrews 1:3).
13: O YHWH, the hope of Israel, all who forsake you will be put to shame; those who depart from you will be written in the earth, for they have forsaken the fountain of living water — combining the living-water image of 2:13 with the Book of Life image. Christ identifies himself as the fountain of living water (John 4:14; Jeremiah 7:37-38), and the Book of Life appears throughout Revelation (Jeremiah 3:5; Jeremiah 13:8; Jeremiah 20:12, 15) as the record of those who belong to Christ. The writing-in-the-earth image resonates with John 8:6-8, where Jesus writes in the dust.
14: Heal me, O YHWH, and I will be healed; save me and I will be saved, for you are my praise — the prayer of total dependence on divine action, not human effort. The centurion in Matthew 8:8 echoes this trust: "only say the word and my servant will be healed." The woman with the flow of blood (Mark 5:34) is healed when she touches Jesus and is told "your faith has made you well" — the sōzein (save/heal) that Jeremiah invokes. The prayer-of-dependence structure is the pattern of all NT healing faith.
15: The mocking question — where is the word of YHWH? Let it come now! — is the scoffers' challenge to the prophet: prove your prophecy by its fulfillment. Peter addresses the same challenge in 2 Peter 3:3-4: "scoffers will come... saying, where is the promise of his coming?" The NT answer is that the apparent delay of fulfillment is divine patience (2 Peter 3:9), not prophetic failure. Jesus endured the same taunt on the cross: "let God deliver him now, if he desires him" (Matthew 27:43).
16: Jeremiah's protestation — I have not run away from being your shepherd; I have not desired the day of disaster — you know what came from my lips; it was before your face — is the prophet's declaration of transparent integrity before God. The motif of the true shepherd who does not run — distinct from the false shepherds who flee — directly anticipates John 10:11-13: the Good Shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep versus the hired hand who abandons them. Jesus fulfills the true-shepherd ideal Jeremiah claims for himself.
17: The prayer — be not a terror to me; you are my refuge in the day of disaster — grounded in the confidence that YHWH, not the attacking enemy, is the prophet's ultimate shelter. Hebrews 13:6 applies this trust-pattern directly: "I will not fear; what can man do to me?" (citing Psalms 118:6). The pattern of taking refuge in YHWH when surrounded by terrifying opposition is the pattern of early Christian witness under persecution — grounded in the same prophetic trust Jeremiah models.
18: The prayer for the persecutors to be put to shame, not the prophet — let double destruction come on them — is the prophetic imprecation that the NT handles carefully. Romans 12:17-21 instructs: "repay no one evil for evil... leave it to the wrath of God" — the vindication is real but belongs to YHWH, not to private revenge. Jesus on the cross fulfills this: he does not call down destruction on his persecutors but prays "Father, forgive them" (Luke 23:34), trusting the Father with ultimate vindication (1 Peter 2:23).
19: The gate of the people — the Benjamin Gate of Jerusalem — where Jeremiah is commanded to stand and proclaim the Sabbath oracle is the public place of maximum visibility. Jesus similarly teaches in the temple courts (Matthew 21:23; John 8:2), choosing public spaces for proclamation rather than private instruction alone. The prophet standing at the gate is the prototype of the apostolic public proclamation — the gospel is not whispered in corners but proclaimed in the public square.
20: The address to kings, all Judah, all Jerusalem, and all who enter these gates — the full spectrum of society from highest to lowest — reflects the gospel's comprehensive scope. Paul's statement that the gospel is the power of God for salvation "for the Jew first and also for the Greek" (Romans 1:16) expresses the same non-discriminatory scope: king and commoner, Jew and Gentile, all are addressed by the same word. The prophetic proclamation to all social strata is the pattern of the apostolic mission.
21: The Sabbath command — take care for the sake of your lives, and do not carry any load on the Sabbath day — grounds covenant faithfulness in its most practical visible expression: Sabbath-keeping. Jesus declares himself Lord of the Sabbath (Matthew 12:8; Mark 2:28), claiming authority over the institution that was the sign of the Sinai covenant. The Sabbath controversy in the gospels is about who defines the Sabbath's meaning — and Jesus's answer is that he himself is its fulfillment (Matthew 11:28-30: "come to me and I will give you rest").
22: The command to not carry a load out of your houses on the Sabbath day and to do no work — to keep the Sabbath holy as YHWH commanded — frames the Sabbath as active covenant sign. Hebrews 4:1-11 reinterprets the Sabbath rest as a theological category: the rest God rested on the seventh day is a rest still remaining for the people of God; Jesus is the one through whom they enter that rest. The physical Sabbath obligation points to the eschatological rest inaugurated by Christ.
23: The indictment that they did not listen and did not incline their ear; they stiffened their neck not to hear and not to accept correction applies the standard covenant-refusal formula to the Sabbath specifically. Hebrews 3:7-8 quotes Psalm 95 ("do not harden your hearts as in the rebellion") as a warning to the Christian community precisely about this pattern. The stiffened neck that rejected covenant instruction is the posture that Christ's invitation overcomes: "learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls" (Matthew 11:29).
24: The conditional promise — if you listen to me and carry no load through the gates of this city on the Sabbath — frames the entire Sabbath oracle as a covenant conditional: obedience opens blessing. Jesus reframes the conditionality radically: not "if you obey, I will bless" but "I have already accomplished everything; now rest in it" (John 19:30: "it is finished"). The conditional structure of the Torah Sabbath is transformed into the completed-work structure of the gospel Sabbath.
25: The promise of kings and princes sitting on David's throne, riding in chariots and on horses if the Sabbath is kept — the continuation of Davidic rule as the reward for covenant faithfulness — finds its NT fulfillment in Christ. The one who sits on David's throne forever (Luke 1:32-33; Revelation 5:5) is the one who perfectly kept the covenant — and through his keeping, opens the inheritance for all who are in him. The Davidic throne promise is unconditionally secured in Christ.
26: The gathering from every corner of Judah — from the cities of Judah, the environs of Jerusalem, the land of Benjamin, the lowlands, the hill country, the Negeb — bringing burnt offerings, sacrifices, and thanksgiving to the house of YHWH is the vision of eschatological worship. The NT fulfillment is the gathering of all nations to the one house of God: Revelation 7:9-12 shows the great multitude from every nation worshipping before the throne. The geographical gathering from all regions becomes the eschatological gathering from all peoples.
27: The reversal — if you do not listen... I will kindle a fire in its gates and it will devour the palaces of Jerusalem and will not be quenched — is the warning that was historically fulfilled in the Babylonian destruction. But the NT reads the unquenchable fire in dual fashion: the historical judgment on Jerusalem (fulfilled in 586 BC and again in AD 70) and the eschatological judgment (Matthew 3:12; Revelation 20:14-15). Between the historical fulfillment and the eschatological one stands the cross — Christ bearing the unquenchable fire so that those who trust in him may be delivered from it.
